coredata.dc:title: Flow injection spectrophotometric determination total antioxidant capacity in human serum samples based on response surface methodology to optimize synthesized peroxidase-like activity carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Guo-Qi
Wei-Wu,
Shi, Yu-Han
Zhao, Yang
Xu, Zhi-Hong
coredata.dc:description: Total antioxidant capacity (TAC) is an important indicator for evaluating oxidative stress of the human body. Since TAC is related to the concentration of reducing substances, it can be detected by using peroxidase-like or oxidase-like activity of nanozyme materials. In this work, the cobalt and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Co/N-CDs) are fabricated for building stability and high peroxidase-like nanozyme through the Box-Behnken design of response surface methodology. The morphology and luminescence properties of obtained Co/N-CDs were characterized by TEM and fluorophotometer, respectively. Interestingly, the surface charge of Co/N-CDs are innovatively investigated by a simple and widespread gel electrophoresis, which holds the potential to be an alternative to Zeta potential analysis. In addition, a flow injection spectrophotometric assay to detect ascorbic acid is develop with a high sensitivity and automation based on a Co/N-CDs/guaiacol/H2O2 catalytic reaction system. The proposed method is also responsive to other reducing substances such as cysteine and glutathione. Therefore, the presented sensor can realize the determination of TAC, and then, some actual human serum samples are detected accurately and quickly (the recovery rates are 93.46–105.61 %).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Flow injection
Total antioxidant capacity
Response surface methodology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of Co/N-CDs catalytic activity by RCFA
RSM to optimize synthesis conditions of Co/N-CDs
Potential detection of H2O2, AsA, GSH and Cys
Determination of TAC in human serum sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from XiHua University Talent Introduction Program(RX2200001988).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: the recirculating-flow detection system for recording Co-N-CDs/H2O2/GA kinetic curves.
Flow injection spectrophotometric system for determining TAC with an Co/N-CDs/H2O2/GA/AsA reaction system.
3D surface response and contour lines using the BBD obtained by plotting: (A,D) Reaction time vs temperature, (B,E) Concentration rate vs temperature, (C,F) Concentration rate vs time.

Characterization of Co/N-CDs. HRTEM images of Co/N-CDs under (A) 200 K, and (B) 600 K magnification. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Co/N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (D) Gel electrophoresis photograph to verify the surface charge of Co/N-CDs under sunlight and UV-light.

Effect of reducing substances on the absorption spectra of 2-PQ. Curve 1: GA + H2O2; Curve 5: GA + H2O2 + Co/N-CDs; Curve 2: curve 5 + AsA；Curve 3: curve 5 + GSH; Curve 4: curve 5 + Cys. The experimental conditions are as follows: 200 μL H2O2 with a concentration of 10 mM; 200 μL GA with a concentration of 80 mM; 2.5 mL PBS buffer with a concentration of 50 mM; reaction temperature was 40 °C; reaction time was 5 min and 100 μL AsA, GSH and Cys with a concentration of 50 mg⋅L−1.

Methodologies evaluation of AsA determination by the FI-SP-TAC system. (A) The recording curves of the repeatability and linearity of AsA determination. (B) The standard curve for the determination of AsA.
Schematic demonstration of optimization of Co/N-CDs synthesis by RSM method and the detection of TAC in human serum.
Effects of interfering substances in human serum on determination of TAC.
Determination results of FIA and SAM methods for AsA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a. ΔA = A0 - A, A0 = 0.967; b.working curve: ΔA = 0.0167c AsA + 0.028.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery results of AsA in dosages obtained by using the FIA method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The concentration values of samples and standards are after mixing by 1 + 1 ratio.

head.ce:title: Flow injection spectrophotometric determination total antioxidant capacity in human serum samples based on response surface methodology to optimize synthesized peroxidase-like activity carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Nankai University, Tianjin 300350, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Nankai University, Tianjin 300350, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Changlong Technology Co. Ltd., Longgang District, Shenzhen 518117, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen Changlong Technology Co. Ltd., Longgang District, Shenzhen 518117, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: these authors contributed equally: Guo-Qi Zhang and Wei-Wu.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A flow injection spectrophotometric determination of total antioxidant capacity in human serum samples based on response surface method.

Total antioxidant capacity (TAC) is an important indicator for evaluating oxidative stress of the human body. Since TAC is related to the concentration of reducing substances, it can be detected by using peroxidase-like or oxidase-like activity of nanozyme materials. In this work, the cobalt and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Co/N-CDs) are fabricated for building stability and high peroxidase-like nanozyme through the Box-Behnken design of response surface methodology. The morphology and luminescence properties of obtained Co/N-CDs were characterized by TEM and fluorophotometer, respectively. Interestingly, the surface charge of Co/N-CDs are innovatively investigated by a simple and widespread gel electrophoresis, which holds the potential to be an alternative to Zeta potential analysis. In addition, a flow injection spectrophotometric assay to detect ascorbic acid is develop with a high sensitivity and automation based on a Co/N-CDs/guaiacol/H2O2 catalytic reaction system. The proposed method is also responsive to other reducing substances such as cysteine and glutathione. Therefore, the presented sensor can realize the determination of TAC, and then, some actual human serum samples are detected accurately and quickly (the recovery rates are 93.46–105.61 %).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Derived from natural UV radiation and its metabolism, the body produces reactive oxygen/nitrogen species[1–2]. At the same time, the antioxidants are secreted to counteract the damage caused by these reactive species oxidizing human cells, which is defined as total antioxidant capacity (TAC)[3]. Obviously, TAC is not only an important source of the human to inhibit oxidative stress, but also a key indicator to evaluate oxidative stress[4]. Generally, these antioxidants can maintain the redox balance of cells and tissues, mainly including glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AsA), etc. However, environmental pollution, poor diet and habits such as smoking, drinking, staying up, etc., make the ability to regulate oxidative stress reduced. Research reports have shown that cancer, arteriosclerosis, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cataracts and Alzheimer's disease are related to the accumulation of free radicals and oxidative stress of imbalance in the body[5]. Therefore, TAC is becoming a crucial biomarker for evaluating the state of human health, and it is of great significance to establish an accurate, sensitive and reliable detection method for TAC.

Since Fe3O4 nanoparticles were found to possess peroxidase (POD)-like catalytic activity[6], many nanomaterials with natural enzymes catalytic activity (named nanozymes) have been developed and widely studied in the fields of diagnosis and treatment, chemical sensing and environmental degradation[7]. Benefiting from the special structure of nanozymes, it not only has excellent anti-interference ability (pH, temperature), but also has the stability that natural enzymes cannot match. In all of nanozymes, carbon dots have been widely studies and applied owing to easy modification, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and simple preparation[8]. Based on easy modification of carbon dots, the strategy of metal-nitrogen doping can provide more efficient catalytic active sites. In particular, the formation of coordination bonds between lone pair electrons of nitrogen atoms from carbon dots matrix and empty orbitals of metal ions can accelerate the electron transfer between the nanozymes and substrates to improve the catalytic performance[9–11]. Inspired by these principles, a strategy of Co/N co-doping carbon dots (Co/N-CDs) was chosen to prepare a POD mimetic enzyme with better catalytic performance[12]. Moreover, the traditional single-factor variable optimization mode cannot test the comprehensive effects of each factor, therefore, we adopted a response surface methodology (RSM) to investigate these factors that significantly affect the POD-like catalytic activity of Co/N-CDs. The RSM method was derived from the idea of mathematics and statistics to establish a theoretical model for optimizing the experimental parameter. This theoretical model could scientifically obtain the experimental factors to optimize the catalytic activity of Co/N-CDs with a less number of experiments[13–15].

In fact, chromogenic products obtained from POD-like catalysis were faded by these reducing substances (such as GSH, Cys, AsA) [16–18]. Based on this extinction reaction and nanozymes with POD-like activity, scientists develop several colorimetric sensors for TAC detection in food matrices. However, studies have confirmed that this fading reaction will re-color over time. Meanwhile, various concentrations of AsA can affect the fading period in the POD-catalyzed process. Obviously, this makes the reaction time a critical factor affecting the detection accuracy in manual operation. Flow injection is a reliable technique that can accurately control the reaction time under the non-equilibrium state [19–22]. It can also provide a support for high-throughput screening owing to the high degree of automation.

In this work, the Co/N-CDs were synthesized from ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid and Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O using a facile one-step hydrothermal. The synthesis conditions of Co/N-CDs were optimized and analyzed by the RSM of Box-Behnken design, which is efficient and more suitable than the widely used central composite design for time-consuming optimizing synthesis process. Meanwhile, the Co/N-CDs prepared under optimum conditions were characterized by TEM and fluorescence spectroscopy to verify the presence of carbon dots. Interestingly, the charged properties of the synthesized Co/N-CDs were verified by gel electrophoresis. And then, an automated flow injection spectrophotometric system for the determination of TAC based on the peroxidase catalytic performance of Co/N-CDs. The proposed strategy accurately controls the Co/N-CDs/GA/H2O2 catalytic reaction process to improve analysis accuracy compared with manual operation (Scheme 1 ). This method showed outstanding selectivity and sensitivity toward TAC and potential application in clinical diagnosis.

A FIA-3100 type flow-injection processor (Jitian, China) and a V-1800PC spectrophotometer (MeiPuDa, China) were formed into the FIA system. The synthetic temperature of Co/N-CDs were controlled by the JC202 electronic constant temperature drying oven (Nantong, China). The Geno Sens 1850 type automatic gel imaging system (Qinxiang, China) and JY600E electrophoresis instrument (Junyi, China) were used to characterize the charged state of Co/N-CDs. The FD-1 vacuum freeze dryer (Boyikang, China) was used to remove the solvent of Co/N-CDs. The morphology and particle size of Co/N-CDs were observed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which is using Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA) and it was operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence spectra were obtained using RF-5301PC-type fluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O, FeCl3, CaCl2, KCl, NaCl, NaNO3, ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na) were purchase from KeLong Chemical Reagent Co. ltd. (Chengdu, China). H2O2(w 30 %), sodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were obtained by YeYuan Biotechnology Co. ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fructose, sucrose, glucose, dopamine, protein, threonine, serine, glycine, guaiacol(GA), AsA, GSH, Cys and 3,3,5,5 tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchase from Aladdin Co. ltd. (Shanghai, China). The solvent used for all preparation reagents are ultrapure water (conductivity < 0.1 μS cm−1) in the whole work.

The Co/N-CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method with little modification [12], replacing EDTA with EDTA-2Na to improve water solubility of raw material. Briefly, 20 mL of water included 20 mM Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O and 20 mM EDTA-2Na. Then, the obtained mixture was transferred to a teon-lined autoclave under 190 °C for 7 h. After natural cooling at room temperature (about 25 °C), a pale pink product was obtained. The solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatants were placed in a dialysis membrane (500 Da MWCO) and dialyzed for one days to get rid of the impurities. The collected Co/N-CDs was freeze-dried to obtain a solid as carbon dots. Since the freeze-dried solid mass of 50 mL of Co/N-CDs was 23 mg, the concentration of Co/N-CDs was 0.46 g⋅L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Co/N-CDs catalytic activity by RCFA
ce:section.ce:para: The Co/N-CDs of catalytic activity was evaluated by a recirculating-flow analysis system (RCFA) which is consisted by a peristaltic pump, a temperature-controlled reaction cell and a flow-through detector (Fig. 1 ). The characteristic of this RCFA is that it can monitor the kinetic process of the dynamic catalytic reaction in real time, which can avoid errors caused by operation or environment, so it can obtain accurate and reliable results[23–24].

According to the definition of POD catalytic activity by the International Committee of Biological Enzymes, that is, the amount of POD required to catalyze 1 μM substrate in 1 min. Therefore, the enzyme activity concentration (E POD) is related to the substrate consumption concentration or the catalytic product generation concentration. After formula derivation, the enzymatic activity can be expressed in this RCFA system by the Eq.1.
(1)
E
POD
=
( Δ A / Δ t )
· K

Among them, the ΔA is the absorbance increment of the catalysate in the linear reaction interval of the kinetic curve; the Δt is the reaction time interval (min) in the linear reaction interval. The K is a constant that only depends on the RCFA.

Fig. 2 shows a schematic diagram of the developed flow injection spectrophotometric system for determining TAC with the Co/N-CDs/H2O2/GA/AsA reaction system, which consists of two peristaltic pumps (P1 and P2), three automatically switchable loops (S, R1 and R2), two reaction coils (RC1 and RC2) for chemical reactions, an air bath for temperature control, a flow-through detector for recording absorbance. The operating procedure of the system are as follows: the carrier of GA pushes the simultaneously injected reagent zone of Co/N-CDs and H2O2 to mix at RC1, occuring a peroxidase-catalyzed reaction to generate a chromogenic product 2-PQ. Meanwhile, the AsA sample is filled with the loop of “S” in the FIA system. After a period of time, the AsA sample zone is injected into the Tween-80 carrier, and brought into the Tee to react with the chromogenic product 2-PQ, and then flowed into the detector to record the absorbance value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RSM to optimize synthesis conditions of Co/N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The RSM was to cross-examine through multiple levels and factors, and the test results were fit by a polynomial equations to obtain the best optimization conditions. Compared with the single factor optimization method and the orthogonal experimental design method, it has the advantages of less number of experiments, rapid modeling, high fitting accuracy and small random error. Therefore, effects of synthesis conditions (i.e., temperature, time, raw material concentration ratio) on the peroxidase catalytic activity was evaluated by the Box-Behnken design (BBD) with three numeric factors and three center points per block. In particular, the peroxidase catalytic activity of Co/N-CDs were evaluated by the ΔA/Δt in catalytic kinetic curve. Since the catalytic activity of Co/N-CDs would be affected by varying preparation conditions, in this study, the BBD of response surface methodology was used to investigate by varying raw material concentration ratio, temperature and time. Table S1 listed the seventeen randomized runs using the BBD program with the corresponding catalytic activity of the preparated Co/N-CDs. Low catalytic activity were observed in low reaction temperature and short reaction time (Runs 1 and Runs 5). It was worth noting that Runs 9, 10, 13 and 14 were obtained excellent catalytic performance. The Optimum catalytic activity (ΔA/Δt = 0.0202) was observed in Run 10, at a consistency of raw materials, a reaction temperature of 190 °C, and 5 h of reaction time.

To build a reliable and accurate model, the Design-Expert 8.0 software was used to process the experimental data from Table S2, and a second-degree polynomial model (Eq.2) that statistically describes the preparation procedure was proposed.
(2) Y = 9.19A2 −5.32B2 −7.02C2 −3.96BC-1.78AC-1.92AB + 0.04C + 9.48B + 0.04A-3.36

Here, the dimensionless variables A, B and C corresponded to temperature, time, and raw material concentration ratio, respectively. And then, the Table S2 was obtained by analysis of variance evaluating this quadratic model. Generally speaking, when F > 10 and P < 0.05 in the Table S2, the proposed fitting equation was in good agreement with the experimental value. The analyzed results of Table S2 from F = 13.34 and P = 0.0013, there was no significant difference between this fitted equation and actual results. Therefore, these results suggest that the proposed Eq.2 could simulate the actual catalytic activity of Co/N-CDs. Finally, by examining the significance of Eq.2 coefficients, effects of A, B, and C on the catalytic activity of Co/N-CDs were not linear, and the order of influence was A (temperature) > B (time) > C (raw material concentration ratio).

Then, 3D response surface of temperature vs time, concentration ratio vs time, temperature vs concentration ratio were obtained respectively (Fig. 3 A, 3B, 3C) using Design-Expert 8.0 software to fit these datas from Table S2. When the 3D-surface response of two independent variables (temperature and time) face downward, it indicated that effect of independent variables and catalytic activity of Co/N-CDs has a maximum value, which is a optimal preparation condition. In addition, the contour map reflects the interaction strength between two experimental factors. Thanks to the contour lines in Fig. 3D, 3E, 3F were elliptical, it indicated that the synthesis time and synthesis temperature significantly affect the catalytic activity of Co/N-CDs. Obviously, the carbonization time and temperature were important conditions for the preparation of peroxidase-like activity carbon dots. Therefore, the optimized preparation conditions of Co/N-CDs were the synthesis temperature of 194 °C, the synthesis time of 7 h and the concentration ratio of 1 + 1, respectively. Under these conditions, Co/N-CDs were synthesized in three parallel experiments, and the results shown in Fig. S1 were obtained. As can be seen, the response signal (ΔA/Δt) related to the catalytic activity was 0.0389 ± 0.0010, which indicated good reproducibility of this preparation method.

Size, morphology and fluorescence properties were important indications of carbon dots. The TEM images in Fig. 4 A showed the Co/N-CDs to be sphere-like with good dispersion. A graphene-like lattice spacing was observed in the Fig. 4B and the XRD pattern (Fig. S2) exhibits a typical broad peak at about 28.9。, associated with the graphitic structure [25]. Their crystalline nature were confirmed by these results. Furthermore, raman spectra data of Co/N-CDs and graphite were compared in the Fig. S3. The results show that both Co/N-CDs and graphite have D peaks and F peaks, which indicated that the Co/N-CDs contain graphite cores. Fig. 4C were the fluorescence emission scanning curve of Co/N-CDs, the results show that Co/N-CDs have the unique excitation dependence of carbon dots. The above results indicated that the Co/N-CDs were successfully synthesized. In addition, the structural characterization data of Co/N-CDs were obtained by previous study[12], such as FT-IR and XPS.

The surface charge of Co/N-CDs were a vital property to study catalytic reaction mechanism. However, the conventional instrument for characterizing the charged state of nanomaterials was the nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer which is not a common instrument in the laboratory. As the highlight of this study, the gel electrophoresis-fluorescence imaging technique was extended to verify the charged state of carbon dots. After the Co/N-CDs and bromophenol blue were mixed, they were quickly injected into the sample well. When the electrophoresis is completed, the negatively charged bromophenol blue can be observed in the gel plate positive electrode under sun light (Fig. 4D). The agarose gel plate was placed in matched fluorescence imaging system, and an obvious fluorescent band derived from Co/N-CDs appeared on the negative end of the gel plate. According to the principle of electrostatic attraction, the Co/N-CDs were a negatively charged nanomaterial, and this result was consistent with that characterized by Zeta potential. Therefore, this facile gel electrophoresis-fluorescence imaging method serves as an alternative strategy to characterize the surface charge of fluorescent materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential detection of H2O2, AsA, GSH and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the peroxidase properties of Co/N-CDs, a Co/N-CDs/GA/H2O2 catalytic reaction system could be used to detect H2O2. As shown in Fig. 5 , the colorless mixed solution of GA and H2O2 were changed to an amber-colored catalytic product (2-PQ) after adding Co/N-CDs, indicating that H2O2 could be measured directly. And then, effect of antioxidant substances on the absorption spectrum of 2-PQ were evaluated by a series of reduction standard samplesa (AsA, GSH and Cys). Thanks to the addition of reducing substances, the 2-PQ was faded can be observed in Fig. 5. Meanwhile, the characteristic absorption of the catalytic product 2-PQ at 470 nm dropped sharply, indicating that antioxidant substances can be detected by spectrophotometry. In addition, it can also be seen that the ability of AsA to fade 2-PQ was the strongest at the same concentration, which indicates that AsA has a strong reducing ability. Therefore, in this study AsA was used to represent the TAC in the sample.

A flow injection technology, which could accurately control the reaction conditions, was introduced to POD-catalyzed system detect TAC in the human serum sample. The strategy for detecting samples in the pipeline of this FIA system was as follows: quantitative injection of Co/N-CDs and H2O2, the catalytic reaction was occured at RC1 driven by GA to generate the 2-PQ color product, which can be used to quantify H2O2; to detect TAC, the 2-PQ generated at RC1 converged with AsA at the Tee, followed by a fading reaction at RC2.

The initial conditions of the FI-SP-TAC system were referenced to our previously report [24]. When optimizing the detection of TAC, R1 and R2 were respectively filled with Co-N-CDs and H2O2, and S was filled with H2O. The response value obtained by the FI-SP-TAC system was marked as A0; under the same conditions, the AsA standard solution filled with S was injected in the current carrying, absorbance value (A) was obtained. Finally, ΔA = (A - Ao) was used to optimize parameters of the FI-SP-TAC system and quantify the TAC. Moreover, the obtaining A0 could also be used to optimize and quantify H2O2.

From the detection principle in Section 3.3, the Co/N-CDs/H2O2/GA reaction system could respond to H2O2, the sensitivity of the system to H2O2 sensing would be optimized first, and then acts as a reagent blank to detect reducing substances. The optimal conditions of the FI-SP-TAC system were as follow (Fig. S4): the loop volumes (R1, R2) were 100 and 100 μL; the reaction coils 1 (RC1) was 100 cm; the carrier C1 was 50 mM GA solution. And then, the experiment conditions of FIA system for TAC are optimized as (Fig. S5): the reaction coils 2 (RC2) was 25 cm; the concentration of H2O2 was 40 mM; the loop sample volumes (S) was 300 μL; the reaction temperature was set to 40 °C by a thermostatic controller.

Under optimized conditions, the precision of the FI-SP-TAC system was investigated using the AsA standards of 5 and 20 mg⋅L−1. The obtained curves are shown in Fig. 6 A left, Through calculations, relative standard deviation of the FI-SP-TAC system was below 0.97 %, which proves the designed system was satisfactory in repeatability. Then, the AsA standards of varied concentrations were injected to evaluate the linearity of the proposed system (Fig. 6A right). The linear response range was 1–40 mg⋅L−1 and the corresponding linear regression equation was ΔA = 0.0176 C AsA + 0.0312 (r = 0.998)(Fig. 6B). Based on the 3 S/k rule, the LOD was 0.85 mg⋅L−1 (S = 0.005, k = 0.0176). Meanwhile, the linear ranges for the determination of GSH(Fig. S6) and Cys(Fig. S7) by this FI-SP-TAC system were 10–50 mg⋅L−1 and 5–50 mg⋅L−1 (ΔA = 0.0113 C GSH − 0.0302 and ΔA = 0.0073 C cys + 0.0584) with the LOD of 1.86 mg⋅L−1 and 1.52 mg⋅L−1, respectively. Furthermore, when the loop of “S”in the FI-SP-TAC system was filled with water, the system can be used to detect H2O2 with the linear range of 4–80 mM(ΔA = 0.0173C H2O2 − 0.0464)(Fig. S8).

Some potential interferences of other molecules (fructose, sucrose, glucose, dopamine, protein, threonine, serine and glycine), and ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, Cl−, K+, Na+ and NO3 −) were mixed into AsA standards of 20 mg⋅L−1 and injected into the system to verify the anti-interference ability of this method. The experimental result was shown in Table 1 . The range of recovery obtained was 90.34–111.88 %, it was indicated that this FI-SP-TAC system was better in selectivity for assaying TAC and satisfying in anti-interference ability, so the proposed method was suitable for the sample matrix of human serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TAC in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: After the human serum samples were pretreated, they were injected into the FI-SP-TAC system for detection. Subsequently, a standard addition method was performed to remove matrix effects. The data obtained was organized into Table 2 , suggesting no difference in the results obtained by above two methods. This indicates that the proposed method was suitable for human serum sample matrix.

In addition, four human serum samples were spiked with various concentrations of AsA standards for recovery experiments to verify the accuracy of the presented method. As can be seen from Table 3 , the recovery data was in the range of 93.46–105.61 %, indicating that this method were accurate and reliable.

In summary, the peroxidase-like activity of Co/N-CDs was optimized by applying BBD model and varying reaction temperature, reaction time and raw material concentration ratio. Based on a gel electrophoresis-fluorescence imaging technique, the surface charge of Co/N-CDs was characterized as positively charged. In addition, a sensitive and automatic FI-SP-TAC system was established by utilizing AsA for a extinction effect of this catalytic reaction of Co/N-CDs/GA/H2O2. The TAC in human serum was determined with satisfying recovery and repeatability. Hence, the proposed methodology possess potential application in clinical diagnosis.

Guo-Qi Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Wei-Wu: Investigation, Methodology. Yu-Han Shi: Software, Visualization. Yang Zhao: Software. Zhi-Hong Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from XiHua University Talent Introduction Program(RX2200001988).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121832.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Beyond oxidative stress: an immunologist’s guide to reactive oxygen species

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Nathan, A. Cunningham-Bussel, Beyond oxidative stress: an immunologist's guide to reactive oxygen species, Nat. Rev. Immunol. 13 (2013) 349-361, 10.1038/nri3423.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Free radicals and antioxidants in normal physiological functions and human disease

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Total antioxidant capacity as a tool to assess redox status: critical view and experimental data

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Twenty-five years of total antioxidant capacity measurement of foods and biological fluids: merits and limitations

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper tannic acid coordination nanosheet: a potent nanozyme for scavenging ROS from cigarette smoke
